{"key_event":"Copperbelt mining boom and labor militancy","reasoning":"Copper revenues, Lusaka’s administrative centralization, indirect rule, and wartime emergency powers raised colonial state capacity from weak-moderate to solidly moderate, though dependence on mining capital and limited rural penetration kept it below strong. African urbanization, worker networks, churches, welfare associations, and the 1935/1940 strikes increased civil-society capacity, but repression, color bars, and incorporation of chiefs meant autonomous African society remained weak-to-moderate.","society_change":0.7,"society_power":4.0,"state_change":1.0,"state_power":6.0}
